step.title: Step 5
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RESET

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over again (game_over=True). The path I was computing seems to be wrong - the player keeps getting stuck. Let me look at the image again - the player is back at the original starting position (top-left blue square). This suggests the game reset when it went into "game over" state.
Wait, looking at the image, the player IS at the top-left position again. The game seems to have reset. Let me re-examine the maze structure more carefully.
The issue is that my tile-based BFS treats all non-black tiles as walkable, but the player might not be able to move through all of them. Let me look at the maze more carefully.
Looking at the ASCII dump from earlier:
- The maze consists of 3x3 tiles
- White (W) and gray (g) tiles form the maze structure
- The player (b) starts at tile (5,5)
- The goal (N) is at tile (15,15)
But the player keeps hitting game_over. Maybe there's a step limit? Or maybe some tiles are walls that the player can't pass through?
Let me re-examine the maze structure. The key question is: what makes a tile walkable vs a wall?
Looking at the ASCII pattern more carefully:
- Row 15-17 (tile row 5): bb|ggg|WWW|BBB|WWW|ggg|WWW|ggg|WWW
- The player is on a blue tile, then there are gray and white tiles alternating
Maybe the maze alternates between walkable (white) and wall (gray) tiles? Let me check:
- If the player is on blue (walkable), then gray tiles might be walls and white tiles might be walkable.
But that doesn't match the maze structure. Let me look at the connectivity differently.
Actually, looking at the image again, the player appears to be at the starting position. The game over might have reset the level. Let me try a different approach - maybe I need to move one tile at a time and check if the move was successful.
Let me try a single move first to understand the mechanics.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game seems to have reset. Let me try a single move to understand mechanics
# First check current player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
player = None
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        player = n
        break

if player:
    rows = [p[0] for p in player['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in player['boundary']]
    pr, pc = sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols)
    print(f"Player at ({pr},{pc}), tile ({pr//3},{pc//3})")

# Try a single RIGHT move to see what happens
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"After RIGHT: game_over={result.get('game_over')}, board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}")

# Check new position
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        pr2, pc2 = sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols)
        print(f"Player now at ({pr2},{pc2}), tile ({pr2//3},{pc2//3})")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Player at (16,16), tile (5,5)
After RIGHT: game_over=False, board_changed=True
Player now at (16,22), tile (5,7)
